dubois-souls-spiritual-strivings.alt: Half-length studio portrait of a young African American woman facing forward and smiling, Georgia, 1899 or 1900
dubois-souls-spiritual-strivings.caption: A studio portrait from W. E. B. Du Bois's Types of American Negroes, Georgia album, the photographs he compiled to present African American life on its own terms, from the same generation and place behind the color line that Of Our Spiritual Strivings describes.
dubois-souls-spiritual-strivings.photographer: Compiled by W. E. B. Du Bois (collector); photographer unidentified
dubois-souls-spiritual-strivings.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (African American Photographs Assembled for 1900 Paris Exposition)
dubois-souls-spiritual-strivings.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions on publication)

dubois-souls-black-belt.alt: A Black cotton sharecropper family standing together in Macon County, Georgia, 1937
dubois-souls-black-belt.caption: A cotton sharecropper family in Macon County, Georgia, photographed by Dorothea Lange in 1937, in the heart of the Georgia Black Belt cotton country Du Bois travels through in Of the Black Belt.
dubois-souls-black-belt.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
dubois-souls-black-belt.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

dubois-souls-black-belt.alt: Black cotton sharecroppers standing outside in Greene County, Georgia, 1937
dubois-souls-black-belt.caption: Cotton sharecroppers in Greene County, Georgia, photographed by Dorothea Lange in 1937, the tenant economy of the cotton Black Belt that Du Bois surveys county by county.
dubois-souls-black-belt.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
dubois-souls-black-belt.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

dubois-souls-dawn-of-freedom.alt: African American children and adults standing outside a Freedmen's school on Edisto Island, South Carolina, c.1862-1865
dubois-souls-dawn-of-freedom.caption: Freedpeople gathered outside a Freedmen's school on Edisto Island, South Carolina, during the Civil War, the post-Emancipation schooling effort at the center of Du Bois's chapter on the Freedmen's Bureau.
dubois-souls-dawn-of-freedom.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (William Gladstone Collection of African American Photographs)
dubois-souls-dawn-of-freedom.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions on publication)

dubois-souls-faith-of-the-fathers.alt: Congregation gathered for a church service in a rural Black church, Heard County, Georgia, 1941
dubois-souls-faith-of-the-fathers.caption: A church service in Heard County, Georgia, photographed by Jack Delano in 1941, the rural Black congregation as the communal center that Of the Faith of the Fathers places at the heart of African American life.
dubois-souls-faith-of-the-fathers.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
dubois-souls-faith-of-the-fathers.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

dubois-souls-meaning-of-progress.alt: A teacher and her pupils inside a one-room rural Black school, Creek County, Oklahoma, 1940
dubois-souls-meaning-of-progress.caption: A teacher and her pupils in a one-room rural Black school, photographed by Russell Lee in 1940, echoing the country schoolhouse and the young scholars Du Bois recalls teaching in Of the Meaning of Progress.
dubois-souls-meaning-of-progress.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
dubois-souls-meaning-of-progress.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)
